title: Steak and kidney pie
subtitle: The proper version: ox kidney, suet pastry lid
excerpt: Chuck steak and ox kidney braised in ale and beef stock until tender, topped with a thick shortcrust lid and baked until golden. The uncompromised version of a British pub classic.
sourceNotes: Classic British recipe documented in Isabella Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, 'Beefsteak and Kidney Pie', which includes the combination of beef and kidney under a pastry lid. Further reference: Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg, on braised beef-kidney combinations.

content.text: Steak and kidney pie asks for patience in two stages. The long

turns tough chuck steak and ox kidney into something silky and soft. Then the rest in the fridge before the pastry goes on. Cold filling under a pastry lid bakes better than hot. The pastry has time to set and crisp before the filling starts to bubble through.

Ox kidney has a stronger flavour than lamb's kidney and is the traditional choice for this pie. If the intensity seems too much, soak the kidney pieces in cold water for 30 minutes before use; this draws out some of the sharpness without eliminating the characteristic flavour that makes the dish what it is.

items.name: Ox kidney
items.prepNote: trimmed of core and cut into 2 cm pieces

content.text: Toss {{beef-chuck}} of chuck steak pieces in the seasoned flour. Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a large Dutch oven over high heat. Brown the beef in batches without crowding the pot, 2 to 3 minutes per side, until deep brown on all surfaces. Transfer to a plate. Quickly brown {{ox-kidney}} of kidney pieces in the same pot, 1 minute per side, and set aside.

Reduce the heat to medium and add the onion to the pot with a splash more oil if needed. Cook for 5 minutes until softened. Add the mushrooms and cook for 3 minutes. Stir in the tomato purée and cook for 1 minute. Return the beef and kidney to the pot. Pour in {{beer}} of dark ale and {{stock-beef}} of beef stock. Add the Worcestershire sauce, thyme, and bay leaves. Bring to a simmer, cover, and braise over very low heat for 2 hours until the beef is very tender. Remove the lid for the last 20 minutes to thicken the sauce. Leave to cool completely, ideally overnight in the fridge.

Preheat the oven to 200°C fan. Spoon the cold filling into a pie dish. Roll out the shortcrust pastry on a lightly floured surface to 4 mm thick. Lay over the filling and trim the edges, pressing to seal against the rim of the dish. Brush with beaten egg. Cut a small steam vent in the centre. Bake for 30 to 35 minutes until the pastry is deep golden. Rest for 10 to 15 minutes before serving.

items.fix: Always use cold filling. The filling should be refrigerator-cold for the best pastry result.
items.cause: Filling was too hot when the pastry went on, causing steam to soften the base
items.symptom: Pastry is soggy on the base

items.fix: Reduce the filling uncovered on the hob before using. The sauce should cling to the meat, not pool at the base of the dish.
items.cause: Sauce was not reduced enough during the braise
items.symptom: Filling has too much sauce and the pastry is lifting

items.fix: Soak kidney pieces in cold water for 30 minutes before cooking. Alternatively, reduce the kidney proportion and increase the beef.
items.cause: Ox kidney has a robust character that not all palates enjoy at full strength

items.fix: Continue braising for another 30 to 45 minutes. The collagen in chuck steak needs sustained low heat to convert to gelatin.
items.cause: Cut into pieces that were too large, or the heat was not low enough
items.symptom: Beef is still chewy after 2 hours

content.text: Steak and kidney pie is one of the classic dishes of the British table. It is mentioned in Dickens and was on pub menus from the Victorian era onwards. Beeton's 1861 manual called for rump steak, kidney, and a thick pastry lid. The same shape appears here, though chuck steak replaced the dearer rump in the twentieth-century home version. The dish has never gone out of fashion. It has simply moved from restaurant menus to home kitchens. It is valued for the same things that make it a slow-cook classic. The kidney seasons the sauce without taking over. The pastry traps the steam from the filling and bakes into something dense and filling.
